title: Production Kubernetes Cluster - Pods CrashLooping (instance 1 in staging)
description: Multiple pods across different namespaces are in CrashLoopBackOff state. Application services are intermittently unavailable.
root_cause: PostgreSQL pod crashed due to OOMKilled (Out of Memory). The database pod had a memory limit of 2Gi but actual usage exceeded this during a batch job execution. When the database went down, all dependent application pods couldn't establish connections and entered CrashLoopBackOff.
resolution:
- Identified OOMKilled in database pod events: kubectl describe pod pod-oomkilled-001-854 -n database
- Immediately scaled up memory limit from 2Gi to 4Gi in the StatefulSet
- Restarted the PostgreSQL pod to recover service
- Application pods automatically recovered once database was accessible
- Added resource monitoring alerts for database memory usage

logs: kubectl logs <pod-name> --previous
Error: Failed to connect to database: connection refused at db.example.com:5432
kubectl get svc -n database
svc-oomkilled-001 is running, but endpoints show 0/1 ready

title: Production Kubernetes Cluster - Pods CrashLooping (instance 31 in billing)
description: Multiple pods across different namespaces are in CrashLoopBackOff state. Application services are intermittently unavailable.
root_cause: PostgreSQL pod crashed due to OOMKilled (Out of Memory). The database pod had a memory limit of 2Gi but actual usage exceeded this during a batch job execution. When the database went down, all dependent application pods couldn't establish connections and entered CrashLoopBackOff.
resolution:
- Identified OOMKilled in database pod events: kubectl describe pod pod-oomkilled-031-667 -n database
- Immediately scaled up memory limit from 2Gi to 4Gi in the StatefulSet
- Restarted the PostgreSQL pod to recover service
- Application pods automatically recovered once database was accessible
- Added resource monitoring alerts for database memory usage

title: Production Kubernetes Cluster - Pods CrashLooping (instance 61 in dev)
description: Multiple pods across different namespaces are in CrashLoopBackOff state. Application services are intermittently unavailable.
root_cause: PostgreSQL pod crashed due to OOMKilled (Out of Memory). The database pod had a memory limit of 2Gi but actual usage exceeded this during a batch job execution. When the database went down, all dependent application pods couldn't establish connections and entered CrashLoopBackOff.
resolution:
- Identified OOMKilled in database pod events: kubectl describe pod pod-oomkilled-061-251 -n database
- Immediately scaled up memory limit from 2Gi to 4Gi in the StatefulSet
- Restarted the PostgreSQL pod to recover service
- Application pods automatically recovered once database was accessible
- Added resource monitoring alerts for database memory usage

title: Production Kubernetes Cluster - Pods CrashLooping (instance 91 in customer-portal)
description: Multiple pods across different namespaces are in CrashLoopBackOff state. Application services are intermittently unavailable.
root_cause: PostgreSQL pod crashed due to OOMKilled (Out of Memory). The database pod had a memory limit of 2Gi but actual usage exceeded this during a batch job execution. When the database went down, all dependent application pods couldn't establish connections and entered CrashLoopBackOff.
resolution:
- Identified OOMKilled in database pod events: kubectl describe pod pod-oomkilled-091-896 -n database
- Immediately scaled up memory limit from 2Gi to 4Gi in the StatefulSet
- Restarted the PostgreSQL pod to recover service
- Application pods automatically recovered once database was accessible
- Added resource monitoring alerts for database memory usage

title: CI/CD Pipeline Failing - Docker Build Timeout (instance 1 in auth-service)
description: Jenkins pipeline consistently failing at Docker build stage with timeout errors. Deployment to staging environment blocked.
root_cause: The Jenkins agent's Docker network was using an outdated DNS resolver that was experiencing issues. npm package downloads were timing out due to DNS resolution failures and slow responses. The DNS server at 8.8.4.4 (configured as secondary) was experiencing regional outages.
resolution:
- Updated Jenkins agent Docker daemon configuration
- Modified /etc/docker/daemon.json to use primary DNS: 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8
- Restarted Docker daemon: sudo systemctl restart docker
- Cleared Docker build cache: docker builder prune -a -f
- Re-triggered failed pipelines - all succeeded

logs: Dockerfile step 'RUN npm install' is taking over 8 minutes and eventually timing out
curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{time_total}' https://registry.npmjs.org/
Connection time: 15.234 seconds (normally < 1 second)

root_cause: The Jenkins agent's Docker network was using an outdated DNS resolver that was experiencing issues. npm package downloads were timing out due to DNS resolution failures and slow responses. The DNS server at 8.8.4.4 (configured as secondary) was experiencing regional outages.
resolution:
- Updated Jenkins agent Docker daemon configuration
- Modified /etc/docker/daemon.json to use primary DNS: 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8
- Restarted Docker daemon: sudo systemctl restart docker
- Cleared Docker build cache: docker builder prune -a -f
- Re-triggered failed pipelines - all succeeded

logs: Dockerfile step 'RUN npm install' is taking over 8 minutes and eventually timing out
curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{time_total}' https://registry.npmjs.org/
Connection time: 15.234 seconds (normally < 1 second)

description: Jenkins pipeline consistently failing at Docker build stage with timeout errors. Deployment to staging environment blocked.
root_cause: The Jenkins agent's Docker network was using an outdated DNS resolver that was experiencing issues. npm package downloads were timing out due to DNS resolution failures and slow responses. The DNS server at 8.8.4.4 (configured as secondary) was experiencing regional outages.
resolution:
- Updated Jenkins agent Docker daemon configuration
- Modified /etc/docker/daemon.json to use primary DNS: 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8
- Restarted Docker daemon: sudo systemctl restart docker
- Cleared Docker build cache: docker builder prune -a -f
- Re-triggered failed pipelines - all succeeded

title: CI/CD Pipeline Failing - Docker Build Timeout (instance 83 in payment)
description: Jenkins pipeline consistently failing at Docker build stage with timeout errors. Deployment to staging environment blocked.
root_cause: The Jenkins agent's Docker network was using an outdated DNS resolver that was experiencing issues. npm package downloads were timing out due to DNS resolution failures and slow responses. The DNS server at 8.8.4.4 (configured as secondary) was experiencing regional outages.
resolution:
- Updated Jenkins agent Docker daemon configuration
- Modified /etc/docker/daemon.json to use primary DNS: 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8
- Restarted Docker daemon: sudo systemctl restart docker
- Cleared Docker build cache: docker builder prune -a -f
- Re-triggered failed pipelines - all succeeded

title: AWS Lambda Cold Start Causing Timeout (instance 1 in customer-portal)
description: Lambda function handling user authentication experiencing frequent timeouts during cold starts, causing login failures.
root_cause: Lambda function had a bloated deployment package (245 MB) with unnecessary data science libraries (pandas, numpy, scipy) that were imported at the module level but only used in a separate analytics function. Cold starts required loading all these libraries into memory, causing 9-10 second initialization times.
resolution:
- Analyzed Lambda function code and identified unused dependencies
- Removed pandas, numpy, and scipy from requirements.txt
- Moved analytics logic to a separate Lambda function
- Reduced deployment package from 245 MB to 15 MB
- Enabled Lambda SnapStart for Java functions (considering migration)
- Configured provisioned concurrency for 2 instances during peak hours
- Reduced cold start time from 9.8s to 1.2s

logs: Cold start invocations: 45% of requests. Average cold start: 9.8 seconds. Warm start: 200ms
Deployment package size: 245 MB. Memory: 512 MB. Runtime: Python 3.9. Heavy dependencies: pandas, numpy, scipy

description: Lambda function handling user authentication experiencing frequent timeouts during cold starts, causing login failures.
root_cause: Lambda function had a bloated deployment package (245 MB) with unnecessary data science libraries (pandas, numpy, scipy) that were imported at the module level but only used in a separate analytics function. Cold starts required loading all these libraries into memory, causing 9-10 second initialization times.
resolution:
- Analyzed Lambda function code and identified unused dependencies
- Removed pandas, numpy, and scipy from requirements.txt
- Moved analytics logic to a separate Lambda function
- Reduced deployment package from 245 MB to 15 MB
- Enabled Lambda SnapStart for Java functions (considering migration)
- Configured provisioned concurrency for 2 instances during peak hours
- Reduced cold start time from 9.8s to 1.2s

title: AWS Lambda Cold Start Causing Timeout (instance 42 in analytics-prod)
description: Lambda function handling user authentication experiencing frequent timeouts during cold starts, causing login failures.
root_cause: Lambda function had a bloated deployment package (245 MB) with unnecessary data science libraries (pandas, numpy, scipy) that were imported at the module level but only used in a separate analytics function. Cold starts required loading all these libraries into memory, causing 9-10 second initialization times.
resolution:
- Analyzed Lambda function code and identified unused dependencies
- Removed pandas, numpy, and scipy from requirements.txt
- Moved analytics logic to a separate Lambda function
- Reduced deployment package from 245 MB to 15 MB
- Enabled Lambda SnapStart for Java functions (considering migration)
- Configured provisioned concurrency for 2 instances during peak hours
- Reduced cold start time from 9.8s to 1.2s

- Removed pandas, numpy, and scipy from requirements.txt
- Moved analytics logic to a separate Lambda function
- Reduced deployment package from 245 MB to 15 MB
- Enabled Lambda SnapStart for Java functions (considering migration)
- Configured provisioned concurrency for 2 instances during peak hours
- Reduced cold start time from 9.8s to 1.2s

title: AWS Lambda Cold Start Causing Timeout (instance 71 in billing)

root_cause: Lambda function had a bloated deployment package (245 MB) with unnecessary data science libraries (pandas, numpy, scipy) that were imported at the module level but only used in a separate analytics function. Cold starts required loading all these libraries into memory, causing 9-10 second initialization times.
resolution:
- Analyzed Lambda function code and identified unused dependencies
- Removed pandas, numpy, and scipy from requirements.txt
- Moved analytics logic to a separate Lambda function
- Reduced deployment package from 245 MB to 15 MB
- Enabled Lambda SnapStart for Java functions (considering migration)
- Configured provisioned concurrency for 2 instances during peak hours
- Reduced cold start time from 9.8s to 1.2s

logs: Cold start invocations: 45% of requests. Average cold start: 9.8 seconds. Warm start: 200ms
Deployment package size: 245 MB. Memory: 512 MB. Runtime: Python 3.9. Heavy dependencies: pandas, numpy, scipy

title: Terraform Apply Fails - State Lock Acquisition (instance 1 in staging)
description: Terraform deployment stuck unable to acquire state lock. Infrastructure changes blocked.
root_cause: A previous Jenkins pipeline job was terminated abruptly (killed during execution) while running 'terraform apply'. The job was killed due to a Jenkins controller restart for maintenance, but the Terraform process didn't gracefully release the DynamoDB state lock. The lock remained in DynamoDB with a stale lock ID.
resolution:
- Verified no active Terraform processes in Jenkins or developer machines
- Checked DynamoDB table for lock entry and confirmed it was stale (2 days old)
- Backed up current Terraform state file from S3
- Executed: terraform force-unlock <lock-id> after confirming safety
- Successfully unlocked the state
- Re-ran terraform plan to verify state integrity
- Applied pending infrastructure changes successfully

logs: terraform force-unlock <lock-id>
Do you really want to force-unlock? This could cause corruption if another process is using the state.
Check CI/CD system and query S3 backend for active operations

The job was killed due to a Jenkins controller restart for maintenance, but the Terraform process didn't gracefully release the DynamoDB state lock. The lock remained in DynamoDB with a stale lock ID.
resolution:
- Verified no active Terraform processes in Jenkins or developer machines
- Checked DynamoDB table for lock entry and confirmed it was stale (2 days old)
- Backed up current Terraform state file from S3
- Executed: terraform force-unlock <lock-id> after confirming safety
- Successfully unlocked the state
- Re-ran terraform plan to verify state integrity
- Applied pending infrastructure changes successfully

logs: terraform force-unlock <lock-id>
Do you really want to force-unlock? This could cause corruption if another process is using the state.
Check CI/CD system and query S3 backend for active operations

title: Nginx Ingress - SSL Certificate Renewal Failed (instance 1 in inventory-mgmt)
description: SSL certificate for inventory-mgmt domain expired. Users seeing 'NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID' errors. Website inaccessible via HTTPS.
root_cause: Let's Encrypt certificate auto-renewal failed because the cert-manager pod couldn't complete the HTTP-01 ACME challenge. Investigation revealed that a recent firewall rule update blocked incoming traffic on port 80 to the ingress controller. The security team had implemented a new WAF rule that inadvertently blocked Let's Encrypt's validation servers (identified as bot traffic). Without access to the ACME challenge endpoint, certificate renewal failed, and the certificate expired.
resolution:
- Immediately identified firewall rule blocking port 80 Let's Encrypt validation
- Coordinated with security team to whitelist Let's Encrypt IP ranges
- Updated WAF rules to allow traffic to .well-known/acme-challenge/* path
- Manually triggered certificate renewal: kubectl delete certificaterequest -n inventory-mgmt
- Verified HTTP-01 challenge completed successfully
- New certificate issued and automatically deployed by cert-manager
- Confirmed HTTPS access restored within 5 minutes

logs: kubectl get certificate -n inventory-mgmt
Status: False, Reason: Failing, Message: Failed to verify ownership of domain
kubectl describe challenge -n inventory-mgmt
Error: HTTP01 challenge failing - unable to reach .well-known/acme-challenge path. Connection timeout to ingress controller.

Investigation revealed that a recent firewall rule update blocked incoming traffic on port 80 to the ingress controller. The security team had implemented a new WAF rule that inadvertently blocked Let's Encrypt's validation servers (identified as bot traffic). Without access to the ACME challenge endpoint, certificate renewal failed, and the certificate expired.
resolution:
- Immediately identified firewall rule blocking port 80 Let's Encrypt validation
- Coordinated with security team to whitelist Let's Encrypt IP ranges
- Updated WAF rules to allow traffic to .well-known/acme-challenge/* path
- Manually triggered certificate renewal: kubectl delete certificaterequest -n dev
- Verified HTTP-01 challenge completed successfully
- New certificate issued and automatically deployed by cert-manager
- Confirmed HTTPS access restored within 5 minutes

logs: kubectl get certificate -n dev
Status: False, Reason: Failing, Message: Failed to verify ownership of domain
kubectl describe challenge -n dev
Error: HTTP01 challenge failing - unable to reach .well-known/acme-challenge path. Connection timeout to ingress controller.

title: Nginx Ingress - SSL Certificate Renewal Failed (instance 60 in customer-portal)
description: SSL certificate for customer-portal domain expired. Users seeing 'NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID' errors. Website inaccessible via HTTPS.
root_cause: Let's Encrypt certificate auto-renewal failed because the cert-manager pod couldn't complete the HTTP-01 ACME challenge. Investigation revealed that a recent firewall rule update blocked incoming traffic on port 80 to the ingress controller. The security team had implemented a new WAF rule that inadvertently blocked Let's Encrypt's validation servers (identified as bot traffic). Without access to the ACME challenge endpoint, certificate renewal failed, and the certificate expired.
resolution:
- Immediately identified firewall rule blocking port 80 Let's Encrypt validation
- Coordinated with security team to whitelist Let's Encrypt IP ranges
- Updated WAF rules to allow traffic to .well-known/acme-challenge/* path
- Manually triggered certificate renewal: kubectl delete certificaterequest -n customer-portal
- Verified HTTP-01 challenge completed successfully
- New certificate issued and automatically deployed by cert-manager
- Confirmed HTTPS access restored within 5 minutes

root_cause: Let's Encrypt certificate auto-renewal failed because the cert-manager pod couldn't complete the HTTP-01 ACME challenge. Investigation revealed that a recent firewall rule update blocked incoming traffic on port 80 to the ingress controller. The security team had implemented a new WAF rule that inadvertently blocked Let's Encrypt's validation servers (identified as bot traffic). Without access to the ACME challenge endpoint, certificate renewal failed, and the certificate expired.
resolution:
- Immediately identified firewall rule blocking port 80 Let's Encrypt validation
- Coordinated with security team to whitelist Let's Encrypt IP ranges
- Updated WAF rules to allow traffic to .well-known/acme-challenge/* path
- Manually triggered certificate renewal: kubectl delete certificaterequest -n dev
- Verified HTTP-01 challenge completed successfully
- New certificate issued and automatically deployed by cert-manager
- Confirmed HTTPS access restored within 5 minutes

logs: kubectl get certificate -n dev
Status: False, Reason: Failing, Message: Failed to verify ownership of domain
kubectl describe challenge -n dev
Error: HTTP01 challenge failing - unable to reach .well-known/acme-challenge path. Connection timeout to ingress controller.

title: Redis Cache Cluster - Memory Eviction Storm (instance 1 in customer-portal)
description: Redis cluster experiencing massive cache evictions, causing customer-portal overload and application slowdown.
root_cause: A newly deployed analytics feature was storing uncompressed temporary computation results directly in Redis cache with long TTLs (24 hours). Each analytics request generated 5-10MB of temporary data. Over the course of 6 hours, this filled Redis memory to capacity. Redis began aggressively evicting frequently-used session and application cache data to make room, causing a cascade failure where applications had to hit the customer-portal for every request.
resolution:
- Identified and immediately stopped the analytics service to prevent further memory pressure
- Deleted analytics temporary keys: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'analytics_temp_*' | xargs redis-cli del
- Freed up 8GB of memory instantly
- Reconfigured analytics service to use customer-portal temporary tables instead of Redis
- Implemented data compression for remaining Redis storage
- Increased Redis cluster memory from 16GB to 32GB per node
- Adjusted maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru with better tuning
- Cache hit rate recovered to 93% within 15 minutes

logs: Redis memory: 15.9GB / 16GB (99.4%). Eviction policy: allkeys-lru. Evicted keys: 127,543 in last minute
redis-cli --bigkeys
Found: session:user:* keys totaling 8.2GB. Largest key: analytics_temp_data (2.1GB)

Redis began aggressively evicting frequently-used session and application cache data to make room, causing a cascade failure where applications had to hit the analytics-prod for every request.
resolution:
- Identified and immediately stopped the analytics service to prevent further memory pressure
- Deleted analytics temporary keys: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'analytics_temp_*' | xargs redis-cli del
- Freed up 8GB of memory instantly
- Reconfigured analytics service to use analytics-prod temporary tables instead of Redis
- Implemented data compression for remaining Redis storage
- Increased Redis cluster memory from 16GB to 32GB per node
- Adjusted maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru with better tuning
- Cache hit rate recovered to 93% within 15 minutes

logs: Redis memory: 15.9GB / 16GB (99.4%). Eviction policy: allkeys-lru. Evicted keys: 127,543 in last minute
redis-cli --bigkeys
Found: session:user:* keys totaling 8.2GB. Largest key: analytics_temp_data (2.1GB)

title: Redis Cache Cluster - Memory Eviction Storm (instance 29 in user-mgmt)
description: Redis cluster experiencing massive cache evictions, causing user-mgmt overload and application slowdown.
root_cause: A newly deployed analytics feature was storing uncompressed temporary computation results directly in Redis cache with long TTLs (24 hours). Each analytics request generated 5-10MB of temporary data. Over the course of 6 hours, this filled Redis memory to capacity. Redis began aggressively evicting frequently-used session and application cache data to make room, causing a cascade failure where applications had to hit the user-mgmt for every request.
resolution:
- Identified and immediately stopped the analytics service to prevent further memory pressure
- Deleted analytics temporary keys: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'analytics_temp_*' | xargs redis-cli del
- Freed up 8GB of memory instantly
- Reconfigured analytics service to use user-mgmt temporary tables instead of Redis
- Implemented data compression for remaining Redis storage
- Increased Redis cluster memory from 16GB to 32GB per node
- Adjusted maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru with better tuning
- Cache hit rate recovered to 93% within 15 minutes

Redis began aggressively evicting frequently-used session and application cache data to make room, causing a cascade failure where applications had to hit the analytics-prod for every request.
resolution:
- Identified and immediately stopped the analytics service to prevent further memory pressure
- Deleted analytics temporary keys: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'analytics_temp_*' | xargs redis-cli del
- Freed up 8GB of memory instantly
- Reconfigured analytics service to use analytics-prod temporary tables instead of Redis
- Implemented data compression for remaining Redis storage
- Increased Redis cluster memory from 16GB to 32GB per node
- Adjusted maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru with better tuning
- Cache hit rate recovered to 93% within 15 minutes

logs: Redis memory: 15.9GB / 16GB (99.4%). Eviction policy: allkeys-lru. Evicted keys: 127,543 in last minute
redis-cli --bigkeys
Found: session:user:* keys totaling 8.2GB. Largest key: analytics_temp_data (2.1GB)

title: Redis Cache Cluster - Memory Eviction Storm (instance 74 in billing)
description: Redis cluster experiencing massive cache evictions, causing billing overload and application slowdown.
root_cause: A newly deployed analytics feature was storing uncompressed temporary computation results directly in Redis cache with long TTLs (24 hours). Each analytics request generated 5-10MB of temporary data. Over the course of 6 hours, this filled Redis memory to capacity. Redis began aggressively evicting frequently-used session and application cache data to make room, causing a cascade failure where applications had to hit the billing for every request.
resolution:
- Identified and immediately stopped the analytics service to prevent further memory pressure
- Deleted analytics temporary keys: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'analytics_temp_*' | xargs redis-cli del
- Freed up 8GB of memory instantly
- Reconfigured analytics service to use billing temporary tables instead of Redis
- Implemented data compression for remaining Redis storage
- Increased Redis cluster memory from 16GB to 32GB per node
- Adjusted maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru with better tuning
- Cache hit rate recovered to 93% within 15 minutes

Each analytics request generated 5-10MB of temporary data. Over the course of 6 hours, this filled Redis memory to capacity. Redis began aggressively evicting frequently-used session and application cache data to make room, causing a cascade failure where applications had to hit the production for every request.
resolution:
- Identified and immediately stopped the analytics service to prevent further memory pressure
- Deleted analytics temporary keys: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'analytics_temp_*' | xargs redis-cli del
- Freed up 8GB of memory instantly
- Reconfigured analytics service to use production temporary tables instead of Redis
- Implemented data compression for remaining Redis storage
- Increased Redis cluster memory from 16GB to 32GB per node
- Adjusted maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru with better tuning
- Cache hit rate recovered to 93% within 15 minutes

logs: Redis memory: 15.9GB / 16GB (99.4%). Eviction policy: allkeys-lru. Evicted keys: 127,543 in last minute
redis-cli --bigkeys
Found: session:user:* keys totaling 8.2GB. Largest key: analytics_temp_data (2.1GB)

title: Docker Registry - Disk Space Exhaustion (instance 1 in user-mgmt)
description: Private Docker registry server out of disk space. Unable to push new container images, blocking all deployments.
root_cause: Docker registry had no garbage collection or cleanup policy implemented. Over 2 years of operation, the registry accumulated thousands of outdated image versions. Old feature branch images, test images, and superseded user-mgmt images were never cleaned up. The registry stored 8,432 image tags totaling 2TB, with approximately 1.5TB being unused legacy images. No disk space monitoring or automated cleanup was in place.
resolution:
- Immediately identified top 50 largest and oldest image repositories
- Coordinated with dev teams to identify which images were safe to delete
- Deleted all images older than 6 months with 'feature-*' and 'test-*' tags
- Ran registry garbage collection: docker exec registry bin/registry garbage-collect /etc/docker/registry/config.yml
- Freed up 1.1TB of disk space, bringing usage down to 45%
- Implemented image retention policy: keep user-mgmt images for 90 days, feature branches for 14 days
- Set up automated daily garbage collection cron job
- Configured disk space monitoring with alerts

logs: df -h on registry server
/var/lib/registry: 2.0T / 2.0T (100%) used
du -sh /var/lib/registry/* | sort -h | tail -20
Majority of space used by image layers. Found 8,432 image tags with many old versions dating back 2+ years

title: Docker Registry - Disk Space Exhaustion (instance 19 in billing)
description: Private Docker registry server out of disk space. Unable to push new container images, blocking all deployments.
root_cause: Docker registry had no garbage collection or cleanup policy implemented. Over 2 years of operation, the registry accumulated thousands of outdated image versions. Old feature branch images, test images, and superseded billing images were never cleaned up. The registry stored 8,432 image tags totaling 2TB, with approximately 1.5TB being unused legacy images. No disk space monitoring or automated cleanup was in place.
resolution:
- Immediately identified top 50 largest and oldest image repositories
- Coordinated with dev teams to identify which images were safe to delete
- Deleted all images older than 6 months with 'feature-*' and 'test-*' tags
- Ran registry garbage collection: docker exec registry bin/registry garbage-collect /etc/docker/registry/config.yml
- Freed up 1.1TB of disk space, bringing usage down to 45%
- Implemented image retention policy: keep billing images for 90 days, feature branches for 14 days
- Set up automated daily garbage collection cron job
- Configured disk space monitoring with alerts

title: Docker Registry - Disk Space Exhaustion (instance 42 in inventory-mgmt)
description: Private Docker registry server out of disk space. Unable to push new container images, blocking all deployments.
root_cause: Docker registry had no garbage collection or cleanup policy implemented. Over 2 years of operation, the registry accumulated thousands of outdated image versions. Old feature branch images, test images, and superseded inventory-mgmt images were never cleaned up. The registry stored 8,432 image tags totaling 2TB, with approximately 1.5TB being unused legacy images. No disk space monitoring or automated cleanup was in place.
resolution:
- Immediately identified top 50 largest and oldest image repositories
- Coordinated with dev teams to identify which images were safe to delete
- Deleted all images older than 6 months with 'feature-*' and 'test-*' tags
- Ran registry garbage collection: docker exec registry bin/registry garbage-collect /etc/docker/registry/config.yml
- Freed up 1.1TB of disk space, bringing usage down to 45%
- Implemented image retention policy: keep inventory-mgmt images for 90 days, feature branches for 14 days
- Set up automated daily garbage collection cron job
- Configured disk space monitoring with alerts

title: Docker Registry - Disk Space Exhaustion (instance 65 in billing)
description: Private Docker registry server out of disk space. Unable to push new container images, blocking all deployments.
root_cause: Docker registry had no garbage collection or cleanup policy implemented. Over 2 years of operation, the registry accumulated thousands of outdated image versions. Old feature branch images, test images, and superseded billing images were never cleaned up. The registry stored 8,432 image tags totaling 2TB, with approximately 1.5TB being unused legacy images. No disk space monitoring or automated cleanup was in place.
resolution:
- Immediately identified top 50 largest and oldest image repositories
- Coordinated with dev teams to identify which images were safe to delete
- Deleted all images older than 6 months with 'feature-*' and 'test-*' tags
- Ran registry garbage collection: docker exec registry bin/registry garbage-collect /etc/docker/registry/config.yml
- Freed up 1.1TB of disk space, bringing usage down to 45%
- Implemented image retention policy: keep billing images for 90 days, feature branches for 14 days
- Set up automated daily garbage collection cron job
- Configured disk space monitoring with alerts

title: Prometheus Monitoring - High Cardinality Causing OOM (instance 1 in dev)
description: Prometheus server repeatedly crashing due to out-of-memory errors. Monitoring and alerting system unavailable.
root_cause: A recently deployed application was instrumented with Prometheus metrics that included high-cardinality labels. The http_requests_total metric included user_id, session_id, and request_id as labels. With 500,000+ unique users and millions of requests, this created an explosion of unique time series (8.5 million for this single metric). Prometheus memory usage grew exponentially as it stored and indexed all these series, leading to OOMKilled crashes every 10-15 minutes. The dev system became effectively unusable.
resolution:
- Identified problematic high-cardinality metrics using Prometheus TSDB status API
- Immediately added metric_relabel_configs to drop high-cardinality labels
- Removed user_id, session_id, and request_id labels from http_requests_total
- Kept only necessary labels: method, status_code, endpoint_pattern
- Restarted application pods to apply new metric configuration
- Prometheus time series count dropped from 15.2M to 45K
- Memory usage stabilized at 4GB (down from 28GB+)
- Rebuilt Grafana dashboards using corrected metrics
- Documented proper metric instrumentation guidelines

logs: Active time series: 15.2 million. Memory in-use: 28GB. Cardinality per metric shows http_requests_total has 8.5M series
curl http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/label/__name__/values | grep http_requests
Labels include: user_id, session_id, request_id, timestamp. user_id has 500k+ unique values

The http_requests_total metric included user_id, session_id, and request_id as labels. With 500,000+ unique users and millions of requests, this created an explosion of unique time series (8.5 million for this single metric). Prometheus memory usage grew exponentially as it stored and indexed all these series, leading to OOMKilled crashes every 10-15 minutes. The dev system became effectively unusable.
resolution:
- Identified problematic high-cardinality metrics using Prometheus TSDB status API
- Immediately added metric_relabel_configs to drop high-cardinality labels
- Removed user_id, session_id, and request_id labels from http_requests_total
- Kept only necessary labels: method, status_code, endpoint_pattern
- Restarted application pods to apply new metric configuration
- Prometheus time series count dropped from 15.2M to 45K
- Memory usage stabilized at 4GB (down from 28GB+)
- Rebuilt Grafana dashboards using corrected metrics
- Documented proper metric instrumentation guidelines

logs: Active time series: 15.2 million. Memory in-use: 28GB. Cardinality per metric shows http_requests_total has 8.5M series
curl http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/label/__name__/values | grep http_requests
Labels include: user_id, session_id, request_id, timestamp. user_id has 500k+ unique values

title: Prometheus Monitoring - High Cardinality Causing OOM (instance 29 in inventory-mgmt)
description: Prometheus server repeatedly crashing due to out-of-memory errors. Monitoring and alerting system unavailable.
root_cause: A recently deployed application was instrumented with Prometheus metrics that included high-cardinality labels. The http_requests_total metric included user_id, session_id, and request_id as labels. With 500,000+ unique users and millions of requests, this created an explosion of unique time series (8.5 million for this single metric). Prometheus memory usage grew exponentially as it stored and indexed all these series, leading to OOMKilled crashes every 10-15 minutes. The inventory-mgmt system became effectively unusable.
resolution:
- Identified problematic high-cardinality metrics using Prometheus TSDB status API
- Immediately added metric_relabel_configs to drop high-cardinality labels
- Removed user_id, session_id, and request_id labels from http_requests_total
- Kept only necessary labels: method, status_code, endpoint_pattern
- Restarted application pods to apply new metric configuration
- Prometheus time series count dropped from 15.2M to 45K
- Memory usage stabilized at 4GB (down from 28GB+)
- Rebuilt Grafana dashboards using corrected metrics
- Documented proper metric instrumentation guidelines

Prometheus memory usage grew exponentially as it stored and indexed all these series, leading to OOMKilled crashes every 10-15 minutes. The staging system became effectively unusable.
resolution:
- Identified problematic high-cardinality metrics using Prometheus TSDB status API
- Immediately added metric_relabel_configs to drop high-cardinality labels
- Removed user_id, session_id, and request_id labels from http_requests_total
- Kept only necessary labels: method, status_code, endpoint_pattern
- Restarted application pods to apply new metric configuration
- Prometheus time series count dropped from 15.2M to 45K
- Memory usage stabilized at 4GB (down from 28GB+)
- Rebuilt Grafana dashboards using corrected metrics
- Documented proper metric instrumentation guidelines

title: Prometheus Monitoring - High Cardinality Causing OOM (instance 68 in dev)
description: Prometheus server repeatedly crashing due to out-of-memory errors. Monitoring and alerting system unavailable.
root_cause: A recently deployed application was instrumented with Prometheus metrics that included high-cardinality labels. The http_requests_total metric included user_id, session_id, and request_id as labels. With 500,000+ unique users and millions of requests, this created an explosion of unique time series (8.5 million for this single metric). Prometheus memory usage grew exponentially as it stored and indexed all these series, leading to OOMKilled crashes every 10-15 minutes. The dev system became effectively unusable.
resolution:
- Identified problematic high-cardinality metrics using Prometheus TSDB status API
- Immediately added metric_relabel_configs to drop high-cardinality labels
- Removed user_id, session_id, and request_id labels from http_requests_total
- Kept only necessary labels: method, status_code, endpoint_pattern
- Restarted application pods to apply new metric configuration
- Prometheus time series count dropped from 15.2M to 45K
- Memory usage stabilized at 4GB (down from 28GB+)
- Rebuilt Grafana dashboards using corrected metrics
- Documented proper metric instrumentation guidelines

title: Elasticsearch Cluster - Split Brain Scenario (instance 1 in auth-service)
description: Elasticsearch cluster split into two separate clusters, each believing itself to be primary. Data inconsistency and indexing failures occurring.
root_cause: Network partition caused by misconfigured network policies in Kubernetes. A recent update to NetworkPolicy resources inadvertently blocked inter-node communication between two groups of Elasticsearch pods in different availability zones. The cluster had 7 nodes, but minimum_master_nodes was incorrectly set to 3 (should be 4 for a 7-node cluster to prevent split-brain). When the network partition occurred, the cluster split into two groups: 4 nodes in one partition and 3 in the other. Both groups had enough nodes (≥3) to elect a master, resulting in two separate clusters operating independently.
resolution:
- Immediately stopped all application writes to Elasticsearch to prevent further data inconsistency
- Identified network partition cause - incorrect NetworkPolicy blocking AZ-to-AZ traffic
- Corrected NetworkPolicy to allow Elasticsearch inter-node communication on ports 9200 and 9300
- Shut down the minority partition (3-node group) to prevent data conflicts
- Verified network connectivity between all nodes: nc -zv <node-ip> 9300
- Reconfigured discovery.zen.minimum_master_nodes to 4 (quorum for 7 nodes)
- Restarted nodes in minority partition to rejoin the main cluster
- Waited for cluster to fully stabilize and shards to reallocate
- Verified data consistency across all indices
- Gradually restored application write traffic

logs: curl http://es-node-1:9200/_cluster/health
Cluster: auth-service-es, Status: green, Number of nodes: 4, Master: es-node-1
curl http://es-node-5:9200/_cluster/health
Cluster: auth-service-es, Status: green, Number of nodes: 3, Master: es-node-5

title: Elasticsearch Cluster - Split Brain Scenario (instance 25 in staging)
description: Elasticsearch cluster split into two separate clusters, each believing itself to be primary. Data inconsistency and indexing failures occurring.
root_cause: Network partition caused by misconfigured network policies in Kubernetes. A recent update to NetworkPolicy resources inadvertently blocked inter-node communication between two groups of Elasticsearch pods in different availability zones. The cluster had 7 nodes, but minimum_master_nodes was incorrectly set to 3 (should be 4 for a 7-node cluster to prevent split-brain). When the network partition occurred, the cluster split into two groups: 4 nodes in one partition and 3 in the other. Both groups had enough nodes (≥3) to elect a master, resulting in two separate clusters operating independently.
resolution:
- Immediately stopped all application writes to Elasticsearch to prevent further data inconsistency
- Identified network partition cause - incorrect NetworkPolicy blocking AZ-to-AZ traffic
- Corrected NetworkPolicy to allow Elasticsearch inter-node communication on ports 9200 and 9300
- Shut down the minority partition (3-node group) to prevent data conflicts
- Verified network connectivity between all nodes: nc -zv <node-ip> 9300
- Reconfigured discovery.zen.minimum_master_nodes to 4 (quorum for 7 nodes)
- Restarted nodes in minority partition to rejoin the main cluster
- Waited for cluster to fully stabilize and shards to reallocate
- Verified data consistency across all indices
- Gradually restored application write traffic

A recent update to NetworkPolicy resources inadvertently blocked inter-node communication between two groups of Elasticsearch pods in different availability zones. The cluster had 7 nodes, but minimum_master_nodes was incorrectly set to 3 (should be 4 for a 7-node cluster to prevent split-brain). When the network partition occurred, the cluster split into two groups: 4 nodes in one partition and 3 in the other. Both groups had enough nodes (≥3) to elect a master, resulting in two separate clusters operating independently.
resolution:
- Immediately stopped all application writes to Elasticsearch to prevent further data inconsistency
- Identified network partition cause - incorrect NetworkPolicy blocking AZ-to-AZ traffic
- Corrected NetworkPolicy to allow Elasticsearch inter-node communication on ports 9200 and 9300
- Shut down the minority partition (3-node group) to prevent data conflicts
- Verified network connectivity between all nodes: nc -zv <node-ip> 9300
- Reconfigured discovery.zen.minimum_master_nodes to 4 (quorum for 7 nodes)
- Restarted nodes in minority partition to rejoin the main cluster
- Waited for cluster to fully stabilize and shards to reallocate
- Verified data consistency across all indices
- Gradually restored application write traffic

title: Elasticsearch Cluster - Split Brain Scenario (instance 98 in customer-portal)
description: Elasticsearch cluster split into two separate clusters, each believing itself to be primary. Data inconsistency and indexing failures occurring.
root_cause: Network partition caused by misconfigured network policies in Kubernetes. A recent update to NetworkPolicy resources inadvertently blocked inter-node communication between two groups of Elasticsearch pods in different availability zones. The cluster had 7 nodes, but minimum_master_nodes was incorrectly set to 3 (should be 4 for a 7-node cluster to prevent split-brain). When the network partition occurred, the cluster split into two groups: 4 nodes in one partition and 3 in the other. Both groups had enough nodes (≥3) to elect a master, resulting in two separate clusters operating independently.
resolution:
- Immediately stopped all application writes to Elasticsearch to prevent further data inconsistency
- Identified network partition cause - incorrect NetworkPolicy blocking AZ-to-AZ traffic
- Corrected NetworkPolicy to allow Elasticsearch inter-node communication on ports 9200 and 9300
- Shut down the minority partition (3-node group) to prevent data conflicts
- Verified network connectivity between all nodes: nc -zv <node-ip> 9300
- Reconfigured discovery.zen.minimum_master_nodes to 4 (quorum for 7 nodes)
- Restarted nodes in minority partition to rejoin the main cluster
- Waited for cluster to fully stabilize and shards to reallocate
- Verified data consistency across all indices
- Gradually restored application write traffic

title: GitHub Actions - Self-Hosted Runner Disk Filling Up (instance 1 in billing)
description: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners running out of disk space, causing build failures and slow performance.
root_cause: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners were not properly cleaning up Docker artifacts after workflow runs. Each build left behind Docker images, containers, volumes, and build cache. Over 3 months of operation without cleanup, accumulated artifacts consumed 475GB of 500GB available disk space. The GitHub Actions runner cleanup service was disabled during initial setup and never re-enabled. Workflows that failed mid-execution were particularly problematic, as they left containers and volumes in partially built states.
resolution:
- Immediately ran Docker cleanup on all runner nodes: docker system prune -a -f --volumes
- Freed up 380GB of disk space (down to 20% usage)
- Re-enabled GitHub Actions built-in cleanup service
- Configured post-job cleanup hook in workflow files
- Implemented automated daily cleanup cron: 0 2 * * * docker system prune -f --volumes --filter 'until=24h'
- Set up Docker storage limits: dm.basesize=20GB
- Configured Docker daemon to automatically remove stopped containers
- Added disk space checks at start of each workflow
- Created monitoring dashboard for runner disk usage

/: 480GB / 500GB (96%). /var/lib/docker: major consumer
docker system df

docker system df
Images: 185GB, Containers: 45GB, Volumes: 89GB, Build Cache: 156GB. Total: 475GB

title: GitHub Actions - Self-Hosted Runner Disk Filling Up (instance 19 in auth-service)
description: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners running out of disk space, causing build failures and slow performance.
root_cause: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners were not properly cleaning up Docker artifacts after workflow runs. Each build left behind Docker images, containers, volumes, and build cache. Over 3 months of operation without cleanup, accumulated artifacts consumed 475GB of 500GB available disk space. The GitHub Actions runner cleanup service was disabled during initial setup and never re-enabled. Workflows that failed mid-execution were particularly problematic, as they left containers and volumes in partially built states.
resolution:
- Immediately ran Docker cleanup on all runner nodes: docker system prune -a -f --volumes
- Freed up 380GB of disk space (down to 20% usage)
- Re-enabled GitHub Actions built-in cleanup service
- Configured post-job cleanup hook in workflow files
- Implemented automated daily cleanup cron: 0 2 * * * docker system prune -f --volumes --filter 'until=24h'
- Set up Docker storage limits: dm.basesize=20GB
- Configured Docker daemon to automatically remove stopped containers
- Added disk space checks at start of each workflow
- Created monitoring dashboard for runner disk usage

root_cause: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners were not properly cleaning up Docker artifacts after workflow runs. Each build left behind Docker images, containers, volumes, and build cache. Over 3 months of operation without cleanup, accumulated artifacts consumed 475GB of 500GB available disk space. The GitHub Actions runner cleanup service was disabled during initial setup and never re-enabled. Workflows that failed mid-execution were particularly problematic, as they left containers and volumes in partially built states.
resolution:
- Immediately ran Docker cleanup on all runner nodes: docker system prune -a -f --volumes
- Freed up 380GB of disk space (down to 20% usage)
- Re-enabled GitHub Actions built-in cleanup service
- Configured post-job cleanup hook in workflow files
- Implemented automated daily cleanup cron: 0 2 * * * docker system prune -f --volumes --filter 'until=24h'
- Set up Docker storage limits: dm.basesize=20GB
- Configured Docker daemon to automatically remove stopped containers
- Added disk space checks at start of each workflow
- Created monitoring dashboard for runner disk usage

logs: df -h on github-runner-01
/: 480GB / 500GB (96%). /var/lib/docker: major consumer
docker system df
Images: 185GB, Containers: 45GB, Volumes: 89GB, Build Cache: 156GB. Total: 475GB

title: GitHub Actions - Self-Hosted Runner Disk Filling Up (instance 84 in billing)
description: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners running out of disk space, causing build failures and slow performance.
root_cause: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners were not properly cleaning up Docker artifacts after workflow runs. Each build left behind Docker images, containers, volumes, and build cache. Over 3 months of operation without cleanup, accumulated artifacts consumed 475GB of 500GB available disk space. The GitHub Actions runner cleanup service was disabled during initial setup and never re-enabled. Workflows that failed mid-execution were particularly problematic, as they left containers and volumes in partially built states.
resolution:
- Immediately ran Docker cleanup on all runner nodes: docker system prune -a -f --volumes
- Freed up 380GB of disk space (down to 20% usage)
- Re-enabled GitHub Actions built-in cleanup service
- Configured post-job cleanup hook in workflow files
- Implemented automated daily cleanup cron: 0 2 * * * docker system prune -f --volumes --filter 'until=24h'
- Set up Docker storage limits: dm.basesize=20GB
- Configured Docker daemon to automatically remove stopped containers
- Added disk space checks at start of each workflow
- Created monitoring dashboard for runner disk usage

title: Kubernetes HPA Not Scaling - Metrics Server Down (instance 1 in customer-portal)
description: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler unable to scale application despite high load. Metrics server pod in CrashLoopBackOff.
root_cause: Metrics server was misconfigured after a Kubernetes cluster upgrade. The new Kubernetes version required additional flags (--kubelet-insecure-tls) but these were not included in the metrics-server deployment. Certificate verification failures caused the metrics-server to crash repeatedly.
resolution:
- Updated metrics-server deployment with required flags
- Added --kubelet-insecure-tls flag for development clusters
- Restarted metrics-server pods
- Verified metrics availability with kubectl top nodes
- HPA began scaling within 2 minutes

logs: kubectl describe hpa my-app
unable to get metrics for resource cpu: unable to fetch metrics from resource metrics API

title: Kubernetes HPA Not Scaling - Metrics Server Down (instance 85 in dev)
description: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler unable to scale application despite high load. Metrics server pod in CrashLoopBackOff.
root_cause: Metrics server was misconfigured after a Kubernetes cluster upgrade. The new Kubernetes version required additional flags (--kubelet-insecure-tls) but these were not included in the metrics-server deployment. Certificate verification failures caused the metrics-server to crash repeatedly.
resolution:
- Updated metrics-server deployment with required flags
- Added --kubelet-insecure-tls flag for development clusters
- Restarted metrics-server pods
- Verified metrics availability with kubectl top nodes
- HPA began scaling within 2 minutes

title: ArgoCD Sync Failing - Git Repository Access (instance 1 in inventory-mgmt)
description: ArgoCD unable to sync applications. All deployments showing 'ComparisonError' status.
root_cause: Organization's security policy rotated all SSH deploy keys, including the key used by ArgoCD to access Git repositories. ArgoCD was not included in the automated key rotation process, leaving it with an expired key. The rotation happened over the weekend without notification to the DevOps team.
resolution:
- Generated new SSH deploy key for ArgoCD
- Added public key to GitHub repository deploy keys
- Updated ArgoCD repository secret with new private key
- Triggered manual sync for all applications
- All applications synced successfully within 5 minutes
- Added ArgoCD to organization's key rotation notification list

logs: ComparisonError: rpc error: code = Unknown desc = authentication required

title: AWS S3 - 503 Slow Down Errors (instance 1 in payment)
description: Application experiencing S3 503 SlowDown errors. File uploads and downloads intermittently failing.
root_cause: Application was storing all user uploads with a date-based prefix pattern: uploads/2024-03-27/. All concurrent uploads were hitting the same S3 prefix, exceeding S3's rate limit of 5,500 PUT requests per second per prefix. The application experienced a traffic spike with 8,000+ concurrent uploads, triggering 503 SlowDown errors from S3.
resolution:
- Implemented hash-based prefix distribution for S3 keys
- Changed key pattern from uploads/DATE/ to uploads/HASH/DATE/
- Hash distributes objects across multiple prefixes
- Deployed updated application code
- Request rate distributed across hundreds of prefixes
- S3 503 errors dropped to zero
- Implemented exponential backoff retry logic for S3 operations

logs: 4xx Errors spiking. Error type: 503 SlowDown. Request rate: 8,000 requests/second to single prefix

description: Application experiencing S3 503 SlowDown errors. File uploads and downloads intermittently failing.
root_cause: Application was storing all user uploads with a date-based prefix pattern: uploads/2024-03-27/. All concurrent uploads were hitting the same S3 prefix, exceeding S3's rate limit of 5,500 PUT requests per second per prefix. The application experienced a traffic spike with 8,000+ concurrent uploads, triggering 503 SlowDown errors from S3.
resolution:
- Implemented hash-based prefix distribution for S3 keys
- Changed key pattern from uploads/DATE/ to uploads/HASH/DATE/
- Hash distributes objects across multiple prefixes
- Deployed updated application code
- Request rate distributed across hundreds of prefixes
- S3 503 errors dropped to zero
- Implemented exponential backoff retry logic for S3 operations

title: AWS S3 - 503 Slow Down Errors (instance 35 in production)
description: Application experiencing S3 503 SlowDown errors. File uploads and downloads intermittently failing.
root_cause: Application was storing all user uploads with a date-based prefix pattern: uploads/2024-03-27/. All concurrent uploads were hitting the same S3 prefix, exceeding S3's rate limit of 5,500 PUT requests per second per prefix. The application experienced a traffic spike with 8,000+ concurrent uploads, triggering 503 SlowDown errors from S3.
resolution:
- Implemented hash-based prefix distribution for S3 keys
- Changed key pattern from uploads/DATE/ to uploads/HASH/DATE/
- Hash distributes objects across multiple prefixes
- Deployed updated application code
- Request rate distributed across hundreds of prefixes
- S3 503 errors dropped to zero
- Implemented exponential backoff retry logic for S3 operations

The application experienced a traffic spike with 8,000+ concurrent uploads, triggering 503 SlowDown errors from S3.
resolution:
- Implemented hash-based prefix distribution for S3 keys
- Changed key pattern from uploads/DATE/ to uploads/HASH/DATE/
- Hash distributes objects across multiple prefixes
- Deployed updated application code
- Request rate distributed across hundreds of prefixes
- S3 503 errors dropped to zero
- Implemented exponential backoff retry logic for S3 operations

logs: 4xx Errors spiking. Error type: 503 SlowDown. Request rate: 8,000 requests/second to single prefix

title: Helm Release Rollback Failed - History Limit Reached (instance 1 in inventory-mgmt)
description: Attempted rollback of Helm release failed. Cannot restore previous working version.
root_cause: Helm release history was limited to 5 revisions (--history-max=5), but team performed 20+ deployments. Older revisions including the last stable version were purged automatically. When the latest release had issues, the team couldn't rollback to a known-good state because history was insufficient.
resolution:
- Deployed from git repository at last known good commit
- Increased Helm history limit to 20 revisions
- Documented current stable release version
- Implemented release tagging strategy
- Added pre-upgrade backup of release manifests

title: Helm Release Rollback Failed - History Limit Reached (instance 84 in auth-service)
description: Attempted rollback of Helm release failed. Cannot restore previous working version.
root_cause: Helm release history was limited to 5 revisions (--history-max=5), but team performed 20+ deployments. Older revisions including the last stable version were purged automatically. When the latest release had issues, the team couldn't rollback to a known-good state because history was insufficient.
resolution:
- Deployed from git repository at last known good commit
- Increased Helm history limit to 20 revisions
- Documented current stable release version
- Implemented release tagging strategy
- Added pre-upgrade backup of release manifests

title: Grafana Alerts Not Firing - Alertmanager Configuration (instance 1 in customer-portal)
description: Critical Grafana alerts not being sent to PagerDuty. On-call team not notified of customer-portal issues.
root_cause: Grafana was upgraded from version 8.x to 9.x, which changed the alerting system from legacy alerting to unified alerting. The upgrade didn't migrate alert notification channels to contact points. Grafana alerts were firing internally but not forwarding to Alertmanager, which meant no PagerDuty notifications were sent.
resolution:
- Reviewed Grafana 9.x unified alerting documentation
- Configured Alertmanager contact points in Grafana
- Created notification policies routing alerts to PagerDuty
- Migrated legacy alert notification channels
- Tested alert flow end-to-end
- Verified PagerDuty integration receiving test alerts

resolution:
- Reviewed Grafana 9.x unified alerting documentation
- Configured Alertmanager contact points in Grafana
- Created notification policies routing alerts to PagerDuty
- Migrated legacy alert notification channels
- Tested alert flow end-to-end
- Verified PagerDuty integration receiving test alerts

The upgrade didn't migrate alert notification channels to contact points. Grafana alerts were firing internally but not forwarding to Alertmanager, which meant no PagerDuty notifications were sent.
resolution:
- Reviewed Grafana 9.x unified alerting documentation
- Configured Alertmanager contact points in Grafana
- Created notification policies routing alerts to PagerDuty
- Migrated legacy alert notification channels
- Tested alert flow end-to-end
- Verified PagerDuty integration receiving test alerts

logs: Grafana shows alerts in 'Alerting' state, but Alertmanager has no active alerts
